competitors.label: HTML data-* global attribute
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for data-* global attributes, dataset access, naming rules, and the role of custom data on HTML elements.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on the data-* attribute model, dataset naming behavior, and the browser API surface for custom element metadata.
theirGaps: The reference is less interview-shaped and less explicit about the common production mistake of treating data-* as hidden application state.

realWorldUseCases: data-* attributes are lightweight DOM hooks for custom metadata, testing selectors, or UI behavior flags that JavaScript reads via dataset.
followUpConfusion: The common production mistake is treating them as private storage or stuffing too much application state into markup.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines data-* and dataset precisely, but it is lighter on the production trade-off between lightweight hooks and leaking too much state into the DOM.
actionableExamples: It includes direct dataset examples and naming guidance, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the follow-up confusions teams hit first: all values are strings, secrets do not belong in the DOM, and data-* is not a state store.

actionableExamples: Keep the practical scenario and the string-parsing warning visible so the page stays operational, not only definitional.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical data-* reference, but our page is stronger as an interview answer because it frames the attribute around the exact misuse developers make in production: treating DOM metadata like hidden app state.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant because attributes, DOM hooks, and basic metadata patterns are core HTML interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong HTML interview-prep source with broad attribute and DOM-fundamentals coverage.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to data-* semantics, so it is less focused on one exact answer about dataset strings and DOM-state trade-offs.

realWorldUseCases: data-* attributes are lightweight DOM hooks for custom metadata, testing selectors, or UI behavior flags that JavaScript reads via dataset.
followUpConfusion: The common production mistake is treating them as private storage or stuffing too much application state into markup.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for HTML prep, but the category page is less explicit about the trade-off between small DOM metadata and overloading markup with state.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong interview bank, so the worked-example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the likely follow-up: dataset values are strings, secrets leak into the DOM, and data-* should stay lightweight.

actionableExamples: Keep the test-hook and UI-config examples visible so the answer stays grounded in everyday frontend use.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad HTML prep, but our page is more specific about the production boundaries that make data-* either useful or risky.

competitors.label: Attributes
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev's attributes module is the strongest third-source comparator for global attribute patterns and for understanding how HTML attributes drive browser behavior.
theirStrengths: It is strong on HTML attribute mental models and on how attributes configure element behavior in practice.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact trivia question and less centered on data-* as a dataset and DOM-hook pattern specifically.

realWorldUseCases: data-* attributes are lightweight DOM hooks for custom metadata, testing selectors, or UI behavior flags that JavaScript reads via dataset.
followUpConfusion: The common production mistake is treating them as private storage or stuffing too much application state into markup.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is useful for attribute mental models, but our answer is more compact about the exact production cases where data-* works well versus where it becomes a misuse.
actionableExamples: The tutorial context remains useful for examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the next question developers hit in practice: how dataset values behave and why secrets or large blobs should not live in markup.

actionableExamples: Keep the dataset-string caveat close to the opening so the page keeps one memorable debugging rule.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is strong on HTML attributes broadly, but our page is better as a short interview answer because it turns data-* into a concrete DOM-hook versus hidden-state trade-off.
